# BMad Method · Quality Analysis: skf-brief-skill

**Analyzed:** 2026-05-02T17:43:02Z | **Path:** /home/armel/Projects/OSS/bmad-module-skill-forge/src/skf-brief-skill
**Interactive report:** quality-report.html

## Assessment

**Good** — `skf-brief-skill` is one of the better-crafted complex workflows in SKF: a clean six-stage discovery arc with strong self-containment, uniform headless gates, an exemplary Invocation Contract block, and a tier-aware feature gating model that all four scanners independently praise. The dominant opportunity is integration debt — step 02's manifest/version/exports detection and step 05's YAML emission re-implement, in prose, work that the shared `skf-extract-public-api.py` and a thin `render-skill-brief.py` would do deterministically — together with a cluster of small contract gaps (no language directives in stage prompts, no idempotency check on existing briefs, no post-write schema validation, implicit headless input/output shape).

## What's Broken

**Bare `_bmad/` reference without `{project-root}` prefix** — `steps-c/step-06-health-check.md:3`
The frontmatter comment reads `(_bmad/skf/ when installed, src/ during development), NOT relative` — the `_bmad/skf/` token is bare, violating the SKF path-standards rule that all `_bmad/` references must be prefixed with `{project-root}/`. This is the single hard failure surfaced by the deterministic path-standards lint.
Fix: change the comment to `({project-root}/_bmad/skf/ when installed, {project-root}/src/ during development), NOT relative`, or reword to avoid the literal `_bmad/` token if it is purely descriptive.

**Terminal step has no progression / completion sentinel** — `steps-c/step-06-health-check.md:23`
Step 06's only body line is `Load {nextStepFile}, read it fully, then execute it.` — no `When complete`, no "workflow done" line. SKILL.md's Stages table labels it "Workflow Health Check / Auto-proceed: Yes" but neither file declares step 06 the terminal stage or surfaces the chain to `shared/health-check.md`. From a static-analysis standpoint, nothing tells the engine "this is where the workflow ends."
Fix: append a Completion line at the bottom of step 06 ("After `{nextStepFile}` returns control, the brief-skill workflow is fully complete — do not re-enter step 05 or step 06") and add a one-line note in SKILL.md's Stages table or Invocation Contract that step 06 hands off to the shared health-check.

## Opportunities

### 1. Integration debt — step 02 and step 05 reimplement work that shared scripts already do (high — 4 observations)

Step 02 walks the LLM through ~120 lines of language detection, manifest parsing, exports listing, and version detection (with workspace fallbacks and pre-release classification). Step 05 then walks the LLM through ~50 lines of conditional YAML emission with five independent inclusion flags and a `target_version == version` invariant. Both are deterministic, both are already implemented (or trivially implementable) in `shared/scripts/`, and both currently cost ~1,200 tokens per invocation while creating LLM-fragility seams that the prompt cannot fully close. This is the single largest lever in the skill — wiring `skf-extract-public-api.py` into step 02 and adding a small `render-skill-brief.py` for step 05 eliminates ~1.2K tokens per run *and* removes a class of mis-classification and mis-emission bugs.

**Fix:** wire `shared/scripts/skf-extract-public-api.py` into step 02 (LLM batches manifest reads, pipes to script in `--mode=quick`, narrates the structured JSON result to the user); add `scripts/render-skill-brief.py` that takes the assembled brief dict on stdin and emits the canonical YAML; add `scripts/validate-skill-brief.py` that runs immediately after the write to check the eight schema rules before health-check.

**Observations:**

- Step-02 §3 + §4 + §4b duplicate `skf-extract-public-api.py` (~700 tokens/invocation) — `steps-c/step-02-analyze-target.md:55-149` (script-opportunities)
- Step-05 §3 conditional YAML emission is deterministic format conversion (~500 tokens) — `steps-c/step-05-write-brief.md:36-99` (script-opportunities)
- No post-write schema validation despite 8 rules in `assets/skill-brief-schema.md` — `steps-c/step-05-write-brief.md:101-106` (script-opportunities)
- Inline YAML template in step-05 duplicates `assets/skill-brief-schema.md` (drift risk) — `steps-c/step-05-write-brief.md:46-99` (prompt-craft M1)

### 2. Headless contract is shallow — automators cannot drive the workflow cleanly (high — 4 observations)

Headless gates are uniformly implemented at every menu (this is a real strength), but the surrounding contract is thin. The Invocation Contract documents only `target_repo`, `skill_name`, `scope_hint`, `language_hint`; step 01 collects `target_version` interactively but it is never reachable headlessly; step 03 still asks for scope-type and boundaries; step 05 emits only a Markdown success message with no machine-readable result; idempotency on existing briefs is unspecified. A CI pipeline that wants to brief 50 packages nightly cannot today distinguish a transient auth failure from a permanent bad URL, cannot pre-supply scope type, and cannot grep a stable result line. The headless level is "easily adaptable" — a small handful of new args plus a final JSON result block transform automation usability.

**Fix:** expand the Invocation Contract Inputs row to include `target_version`, `source_authority`, `scope_type`, `include`, `exclude`, `scripts_intent`, `assets_intent`, `intent`; consume them at the relevant gates; emit a final fenced JSON block in step 05 when `headless_mode` is true (`{status, brief_path, skill_name, version, language, scope_type, halt_reason}`); add an explicit overwrite policy with a `--force`-style headless behavior.

**Observations:**

- Headless input contract documents only 4 args; step 01 collects `target_version` with no headless path — `SKILL.md:41` and `steps-c/step-01-gather-intent.md:89-107` (prompt-craft L4, enhancement Headless table)
- No structured output / JSON result block — automators cannot parse outcomes — `steps-c/step-05-write-brief.md` (enhancement H4)
- No idempotency / overwrite policy on existing `skill-brief.yaml` — `steps-c/step-05-write-brief.md` §2-§4 (enhancement H2, cohesion #2)
- Scope type, include/exclude, scripts/assets intent all lack headless args — `steps-c/step-03-scope-definition.md` (enhancement Headless table)

### 3. Self-containment gap — language directive lives only in SKILL.md (medium — 7 observations)

SKILL.md's Workflow Rules block instructs "Always communicate in `{communication_language}`", but none of the six stage files reference the variable. Under context compaction, SKILL.md instructions are the most likely thing to drop — and once dropped, the step prompts have no surviving language directive. Step 05 is especially exposed because it writes a YAML artifact whose `description` and `notes` fields should honor `{document_output_language}` but no step-level directive exists. This is the exact self-containment failure mode the prompt-craft scanner spec calls out, and the path-standards lint flags six matching `config-header` issues.

**Fix:** add a one-line directive to each step's `Rules` block: `All user-facing output in {communication_language}.` For step 05 also add `{document_output_language}` for the written artifact. Costs <10 tokens per file.

**Observations:**

- 0/6 step files reference `{communication_language}` — `steps-c/step-01..06` (prompt-craft M2)
- step-01 line 1 — no config header (workflow-integrity prepass)
- step-02 line 1 — no config header (workflow-integrity prepass)
- step-03 line 1 — no config header (workflow-integrity prepass)
- step-04 line 1 — no config header (workflow-integrity prepass)
- step-05 line 1 — no config header (workflow-integrity prepass)
- step-06 line 1 — no config header (workflow-integrity prepass)

### 4. Asymmetric HITL gates — the workflow protects against bad input but doesn't help when input is broken (medium — 6 observations)

Three HALT conditions and several confirmation gates exist throughout the workflow, but none of them diagnose what's wrong when something fails. Doc URLs are collected without being fetched (a typo silently bakes into the brief and surfaces hours later in `skf-create-skill`). `gh auth` failures say "ensure gh auth is configured" without running `gh auth status`. Truncated trees warn "may require local clone" without offering to clone. Monorepos are analyzed at the root with no "which workspace?" question. Skill-name proposals don't check for collisions. Each gate that today says "are you sure?" should also say "here's what I noticed that might be wrong" — that asymmetry is the dominant friction shape across the user journeys.

**Fix:** add diagnostic-on-failure behavior at the existing gates: HEAD-check doc URLs in step 02 before write; auto-run `gh auth status` on `gh api` failure; offer `[L] Clone locally` on truncated trees; add a "which workspace?" prompt when the root manifest declares `workspaces`; check `~/.claude/skills/` for name collisions in step 01 §6.

**Observations:**

- Doc URLs never verified before brief is written — `steps-c/step-01-gather-intent.md` §3, `steps-c/step-03-scope-definition.md` §2b (enhancement H1)
- `gh auth` failure doesn't surface `gh auth status` — `steps-c/step-02-analyze-target.md` §1 (enhancement M4)
- Truncated tree leaves user holding the bag — `steps-c/step-02-analyze-target.md` §1 (enhancement M5)
- Monorepo workspace not disambiguated — `steps-c/step-02-analyze-target.md` §3-§4 (enhancement M1, edge-case journey)
- Skill-name collision not checked — `steps-c/step-01-gather-intent.md` §6 (enhancement M6)
- Forge-tier corrupted YAML behavior unspecified — `steps-c/step-01-gather-intent.md` §1 (enhancement L5, hostile-environment journey)

### 5. Description and version provenance are spread across multiple steps with no single source of truth (medium — 3 observations)

Two pieces of brief content are derived implicitly across multiple steps with no explicit synthesis: the schema-required `description` field (1-3 sentences, surfaces in skill registries) is captured as free-form intent in step 01 and "derived" in step 04 with no derivation prompt or rule, and the `version` field is shaped across step 01 §3b (gather), step 02 §4b (auto-detect), and step 05 §3 (resolve). Both are correct in steady state, but the rules for each are spread across three files, which creates drift risk and means re-entering any of those steps loses prior judgment.

**Fix:** add an explicit description-synthesis step (either at end of step 01 so the user confirms the synthesized text before analysis, or as a Step 4.0 with a one-shot derivation prompt). Consolidate version-resolution rules into `references/version-resolution.md` and have steps 01/02/05 load and reference the same file.

**Observations:**

- Schema requires 1-3 sentence `description`, but synthesis is implicit — `steps-c/step-04-confirm-brief.md` §4 vs `steps-c/step-01-gather-intent.md` §4 (cohesion #1)
- Version resolution logic spread across three files (drift risk) — `steps-c/step-01-gather-intent.md:89-107`, `steps-c/step-02-analyze-target.md:127-149`, `steps-c/step-05-write-brief.md:36-99` (cohesion #5)
- Re-entering step 03 from step 04 [R] doesn't specify whether prior selections are preserved — `steps-c/step-03-scope-definition.md:18` and `steps-c/step-04-confirm-brief.md` (cohesion #3)

## Strengths

- **Frontmatter is exemplary** — name matches folder, two-part description with quoted trigger phrases, no extra fields. (`SKILL.md:1-4`)
- **Invocation Contract block** is unusual and high-signal — Inputs/Gates/Outputs/Headless in one table. Worth keeping as a pattern. (`SKILL.md:39-44`)
- **Role section as compact theory-of-mind** — "skill scoping architect collaborating with a developer… work together as equals" establishes collaboration model in one sentence. (`SKILL.md:12-14`)
- **Self-containment** — every step file re-states gate contracts locally; no "see SKILL.md" back-references (residual gap is `{communication_language}` only).
- **Uniform headless gate handling** — every interactive menu carries `GATE [default: X]` annotations and explicit log lines; composes cleanly with workflow-runner.
- **Discipline of single-responsibility steps** — every step's `Rules` block explicitly states what it is *not* responsible for ("do not analyze the repo yet", "do not write files yet", "do not make scope decisions unilaterally").
- **Tier-aware feature gating** — CCC at step 02 (Forge+/Deep), QMD at step 05 (Deep) — both gated cleanly, both fail-safe (skip silently when unavailable).
- **Docs-only branch is genuinely first-class** — threaded through steps 01/02/03/05 without if-else clutter dominating the prose.
- **Linear stage graph is correct for an interactive scoping workflow** — pre-pass found zero structural issues; no parallelism is meaningful here.
- **Validation gates placed before expensive work** — `forge-tier.yaml` at step 01, repo accessibility at step 02. Fail-fast done right.
- **Step-05 chain-to-health-check coda** with the explicit "summary is NOT the terminal step" guard — addresses a real failure mode where an LLM sees a final-looking message and stops. (`steps-c/step-05-write-brief.md:186-193`)
- **Step-05 §3 version-resolution invariant** — "`target_version` and `version` must never carry different values" — exactly the kind of invariant that prevents subtle downstream bugs.
- **Intent-Before-Ingestion pattern** — step 01 gathers intent *before* step 02 analyzes the repo. Strong facilitative pattern.
- **Stage chain integrity** — every `nextStepFile` resolves to a real file, including the relative-path comment in step 06 explaining how `shared/health-check.md` resolves against the SKF module root.

## Detailed Analysis

### Structure & Integrity

Structurally sound: complex 6-stage workflow with coherent SKILL.md, all referenced stage files present (0 missing, 0 orphaned), sequential `step-01-..step-06-` naming, explicit `nextStepFile` chain, all required SKILL.md sections present in logical order. Two real issues: the bare `_bmad/` reference in step 06 (path-standards lint hard fail) and the terminal step's missing completion sentinel. Six medium config-header findings and six low "polite please" findings are also flagged but covered by themes 3 and a stylistic cleanup.

### Craft & Writing Quality

One of the better-crafted skills in the SKF set. SKILL.md is lean (54 lines, ~660 tokens), well-routed, and carries a strong Role section that doubles as theory-of-mind. All six stage prompts have `STEP GOAL`, `Rules`, `MANDATORY SEQUENCE`, `MENU OPTIONS` with explicit headless `GATE` defaults, and a `CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE`. Self-containment is strong (no "see SKILL.md" back-references). Two unique findings beyond themes 1/3: the Overview is thin for the workflow's complexity (could expand from 3 to 6-8 lines with domain framing for "what makes a good skill brief" and why brief-skill is split from create-skill), and the prepass scanner missed the `steps-c/` directory (tool issue, not a skill issue).

- **overview_quality:** appropriate-but-thin — covers what the skill produces but not what makes a good brief or why it's split from create-skill
- **progressive_disclosure:** good — scope templates and brief schema correctly extracted to `assets/`

### Cohesion & Design

Coherent gather-analyze-define-confirm-write arc with strong dimension scores: stage flow strong, purpose alignment strong (with one medium gap on description derivation), complexity appropriate (six stages and three gates is well-calibrated for an upfront design session), dependency graph strong (linear is correct here), external skill integration strong (CCC, QMD, advanced-elicitation, party-mode all gated cleanly). Moderate gap-and-redundancy score driven by the description/version provenance issues in theme 5 and the missing idempotency check in theme 2.

- **stage_flow:** strong — textbook discovery arc, each step produces what the next consumes, `nextStepFile` (not free-form "go to step N") is execution-engine-friendly
- **purpose_alignment:** strong-with-gap — Role/description honored throughout; description-derivation gap is the medium issue
- **complexity:** strong — six stages + three gates is well-calibrated for the artifact's importance
- **gap_redundancy:** moderate — idempotency, post-write validation, description synthesis, version-resolution sprawl
- **dependency_graph:** strong — linear chain with one human-controlled back-edge from step 04 → step 03
- **external_integration:** strong — every external skill call is gated and fail-safe

### Execution Efficiency

Fundamentally an interactive scoping conversation; high-impact subagent / parallelization patterns largely don't apply. Pre-pass found zero structural issues. Three medium opportunities: step 02 sequential probes that could batch their manifest reads (theme 1 absorbs this — the script wiring solves it more thoroughly), auto-detect version always running even when `target_version` is authoritative (~1-2 redundant calls per invocation when user has specified a version), and `assets/skill-brief-schema.md` loaded twice across steps 04 and 05 with no caching hint. CCC semantic discovery could run in parallel with the static probes in step 02 (small wall-clock savings on Forge+/Deep tiers).

### User Experience

Six user journeys (first-timer, expert, confused, edge-case, hostile-environment, automator) reveal a consistent friction shape: HITL gates protect against bad input but don't diagnose when input is broken (theme 4), and there is no "skip-to-build" entry point for experts who already have a draft. The expert journey is poorly served — even with `--headless`, every gate must be walked through with no `--from-yaml=draft.yaml` shortcut. The automator's journey is documented as "easily adaptable" but the contract today is implicit (theme 2).

- **autonomous.potential:** easily-adaptable — every gate has `headless_mode` checks, but the input/output contract is shallow (4 documented inputs, no JSON output, no documented exit codes, no idempotency policy)
- **autonomous.notes:** richer headless arg surface plus a final fenced JSON result block plus an explicit overwrite policy lifts this to headless-ready

Notable journey-specific observations:

- First-timer hits a wall on missing `forge-tier.yaml` (HALT with no recovery path) and a cold scope-type menu that explains nothing inline

### Script Opportunities

Brief-skill ships zero scripts of its own but sits on a rich shared toolbox at `src/shared/scripts/`. The dial is integration debt, not missing functionality.

- Finding 1 (HIGH, ~700 tokens): wire `skf-extract-public-api.py` into step 02 §3/§4/§4b
- Finding 2 (HIGH, ~500 tokens): new `scripts/render-skill-brief.py` for step 05 §3
- Finding 3 (HIGH, correctness): new `scripts/validate-skill-brief.py` between step 05 write and health-check
- Finding 4 (MEDIUM, ~200 tokens): tree summarizer (or extend `skf-extract-public-api.py` with `--list-tree`)

- Finding 6 (MEDIUM, ~120 tokens): brief-assembly dict construction (collapses into Finding 2)

## Recommendations

1. **Wire the existing shared scripts into step 02 and add `render-skill-brief.py` + `validate-skill-brief.py`.** Resolves theme 1 (4 observations, ~1,200 tokens/invocation, plus a class of correctness bugs). Highest leverage by a wide margin — most of the work is already done in `src/shared/scripts/`. Effort: medium.
2. **Fix the bare `_bmad/` path comment in step 06 and add a Completion sentinel.** Closes the only deterministic lint failure and the structural progression gap. Effort: low.
3. **Expand the headless contract — new args, a final JSON result block, and an explicit overwrite policy.** Resolves theme 2 (4 observations). Lifts the skill from "easily adaptable" to "headless-ready" and unblocks CI/automation use cases. Effort: medium.
4. **Add `All user-facing output in {communication_language}` to each step's `Rules` block (and `{document_output_language}` to step 05).** Resolves theme 3 (7 observations). Eliminates the self-containment failure mode under context compaction. Effort: low.
5. **Convert HALTs to recoverable prompts and add diagnostic-on-failure behavior at gates** (HEAD-check doc URLs, auto-run `gh auth status`, offer local clone on truncation, monorepo workspace prompt, skill-name collision check). Resolves theme 4 (6 observations). Effort: medium.
6. **Add an explicit description-synthesis step and consolidate version-resolution into `references/version-resolution.md`.** Resolves theme 5 (3 observations). Effort: low.
7. **Expand SKILL.md Overview from 3 to 6-8 lines** with domain framing for "what makes a good skill brief" and theory-of-mind for the user. Effort: low.

---
name: skf-brief-skill
description: Design a skill scope through guided discovery. Use when the user requests to "create a skill brief" or "brief a skill."
---

# Brief Skill

## Overview

Helps the user define what to skill — target repo, scope, language, inclusion/exclusion patterns — and produces a skill-brief.yaml that drives create-skill. This is the first step in the skill creation pipeline. The brief becomes the input contract for create-skill, which performs the actual compilation.

## Role

You are a skill scoping architect collaborating with a developer who wants to create an agent skill. You bring expertise in source code analysis, API surface identification, and skill boundary design, while the user brings their domain knowledge and specific use case. Work together as equals.

## Workflow Rules

These rules apply to every step in this workflow:

- Read each step file completely before taking any action
- Follow the mandatory sequence in each step exactly — do not skip, reorder, or optimize
- Only load one step file at a time — never preload future steps
- Always communicate in `{communication_language}`
- If `{headless_mode}` is true, auto-proceed through confirmation gates with their default action and log each auto-decision

## Stages

| # | Step | File | Auto-proceed |
|---|------|------|--------------|
| 1 | Gather Intent | steps-c/step-01-gather-intent.md | No (interactive) |
| 2 | Analyze Target | steps-c/step-02-analyze-target.md | Yes |
| 3 | Scope Definition | steps-c/step-03-scope-definition.md | No (interactive) |
| 4 | Confirm Brief | steps-c/step-04-confirm-brief.md | No (confirm) |
| 5 | Write Brief | steps-c/step-05-write-brief.md | Yes |
| 6 | Workflow Health Check | steps-c/step-06-health-check.md | Yes |

## Invocation Contract

| Aspect | Detail |
|--------|--------|
| **Inputs** | target_repo [required], skill_name [required], scope_hint [optional], language_hint [optional] |
| **Gates** | step-01: Input Gate [use args] | step-03: Confirm Gate [C] | step-04: Confirm Gate [C] |
| **Outputs** | skill-brief.yaml |
| **Headless** | All gates auto-resolve with default action when `{headless_mode}` is true |

## On Activation

1. Load config from `{project-root}/_bmad/skf/config.yaml` and resolve:
   - `project_name`, `output_folder`, `user_name`, `communication_language`, `forge_data_folder`, `sidecar_path`

2. **Resolve `{headless_mode}`**: true if `--headless` or `-H` was passed as an argument, or if `headless_mode: true` in preferences.yaml. Default: false.

3. Load, read the full file, and then execute `./steps-c/step-01-gather-intent.md` to begin the workflow.

5	# Step 2: Analyze Target
6	
7	## STEP GOAL:
8	
9	To analyze the target repository by resolving its location, reading its structure, detecting the primary language, and listing top-level modules and exports — providing the user with a factual foundation for scoping decisions.
10	
11	## Rules
12	
13	- Focus only on analysis — do not define scope yet (Step 03)
14	- Do not make scoping decisions or recommendations
15	- Do not hallucinate or guess about repository contents
16	
17	## MANDATORY SEQUENCE
18	
19	**CRITICAL:** Follow this sequence exactly. Do not skip, reorder, or improvise unless user explicitly requests a change.
20	
21	### 1. Resolve Target Location
22	
23	**For GitHub URLs:**
24	- Use `gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}` to verify the repository exists
25	- Use `gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/git/trees/HEAD?recursive=1` to get the file tree
26	
27	**Truncation detection:** After receiving the tree response, check the `truncated` field in the JSON output. If `truncated: true`:
28	- Display: "Note: GitHub API returned a truncated tree response ({count} items). Full analysis may require a local clone."
29	- Record in analysis summary: "Tree listing is partial — some files may not appear in the analysis."
30	- For very large repos (>1000 files in tree response): suggest local clone for complete analysis.
31	
32	- If inaccessible: **HALT** — "**Error:** Cannot access repository at {url}. Please verify the URL is correct and you have access. If private, ensure `gh auth` is configured."
33	
34	**For local paths:**
35	- Verify the directory exists
36	- List the directory tree
37	- If path doesn't exist: **HALT** — "**Error:** Directory not found at {path}. Please verify the path is correct."
38	
39	Display: "**Resolving target...**"
40	
41	### 2. Read Repository Structure
42	
43	List the top-level directory structure:
44	
45	"**Repository Structure:**
46	```
47	{repo-name}/
48	├── {top-level files}
49	├── {top-level directories}/
50	│   └── ...
51	└── ...
52	```
53	**Total:** {file count} files, {directory count} directories"
54	
55	### 3. Detect Primary Language
56	
57	Examine file extensions and configuration files to detect the primary language:
58	
59	**Detection signals (check in order):**
60	1. `package.json` → JavaScript/TypeScript
61	2. `tsconfig.json` → TypeScript
62	3. `Cargo.toml` → Rust
63	4. `pyproject.toml` or `setup.py` or `setup.cfg` → Python
64	5. `go.mod` → Go
65	6. `pom.xml` or `build.gradle` → Java
66	7. `*.csproj` or `*.sln` → C#
67	8. `Gemfile` → Ruby
68	9. File extension frequency analysis as fallback
69	
70	"**Detected language:** {language}
71	**Confidence:** {high/medium/low}
72	**Detection source:** {what config file or pattern confirmed it}"
73	
74	If confidence is low or ambiguous: flag for user override in step 03.
75	
76	### 4. List Top-Level Modules and Exports
77	
78	Based on detected language, identify public API surface:
79	
80	**For JavaScript/TypeScript:**
81	- Check `package.json` for `main`, `exports`, `module` fields
82	- Look for `index.ts`/`index.js` in `src/`
83	- List directories under `src/` as potential modules
84	
85	**For Python:**
86	- Check `__init__.py` files for public exports
87	- List top-level packages under the source directory
88	
89	**For Rust:**
90	- Check `lib.rs` for `pub mod` declarations
91	- List modules from `src/` directory
92	
93	**For other languages:**
94	- List top-level source directories as potential modules
95	- Note any obvious entry points
96	
97	"**Top-Level Modules/Directories:**
98	{numbered list of modules with brief description of each}
99	
100	**Detected Exports/Entry Points:**
101	{numbered list of public-facing items found}"
102	
103	**Semantic Signals (Forge+ and Deep with ccc only):**
104	
105	**Remote source guard:** If the target source was resolved via GitHub API (remote URL, not a local file path), skip this CCC subsection — CCC requires a local source index and cannot operate on remote-only sources. Note: "CCC semantic discovery skipped — target is remote. CCC discovery will run automatically during create-skill after the source is cloned."
106	
107	If `tools.ccc` is true in forge-tier.yaml, supplement the module listing with a semantic discovery pass:
108	
109	**CCC Semantic Discovery:**
110	- **Claude Code:** Use `/ccc search "{repo_name} public API exports modules" {source_path}`
111	- **Cursor:** Use `ccc` MCP server `search` tool with query `"{repo_name} public API exports modules"` and path `{source_path}`
112	- **CLI fallback:** `ccc search "{repo_name} public API exports modules" --path {source_path} --limit 10`
113	
114	See `knowledge/tool-resolution.md` for full bridge-to-tool mapping.
115	
116	If results are returned, display:
117	
118	"**Semantic Signals (ccc):**
119	{numbered list of file:snippet pairs from CCC results — top 5 most relevant}"
120	
121	This supplements — never replaces — the explicit module list above. CCC may surface non-obvious entry points (dynamically constructed exports, re-export chains) that static directory analysis misses.
122	
123	If CCC is unavailable or returns no results: skip this subsection silently.
124	
125	### 4b. Detect Source Version
126	
127	**If `target_version` was provided in step 01:**
128	- Display: "**Target version:** {target_version} (user-specified)"
129	- Still run auto-detection below for informational purposes.
130	
131	Attempt to auto-detect the source version using the rules from the skill-brief-schema.md Version Detection section:
132	
133	**For Python:** Check `pyproject.toml` `[project] version` (static) → if `dynamic = ["version"]`, check `__init__.py` for `__version__` → `_version.py` if exists → `setup.py` `version=` → `git describe --tags --abbrev=0`
134	**For JavaScript/TypeScript:** Check root `package.json` `"version"` field → if root has `"private": true` with a `"workspaces"` array or lacks a `"version"` field, fall back to a primary workspace package's `package.json` (e.g., `code/core/package.json`, or the first matching `packages/*/package.json`). For GitHub sources, prefer `gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/releases/latest` → `tag_name` when a non-pre-release tag exists, over a default-branch pre-release. Treat a version containing `-alpha`, `-beta`, `-rc`, `-next`, or `-canary` as a pre-release.
135	**For Rust:** Check `Cargo.toml` `[package] version` (static) → if `version = { workspace = true }`, resolve from workspace root `Cargo.toml` → `git describe --tags --abbrev=0`
136	**For Go:** Check `go.mod` or `git describe --tags --abbrev=0`
137	
138	**For GitHub repos:** Use `gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/contents/{file}` to read version files (decode base64 content).
139	**For local repos:** Read the file directly.
140	
141	Display: "**Detected version:** {version or 'Not detected — will default to 1.0.0'}"
142	
143	{If target_version was provided AND auto-detected version differs:}
144	"**Note:** Detected version ({detected_version}) differs from your target version ({target_version}). Using target version."
145	
146	{If target_version was provided:}
147	Store `target_version` as the brief's `version` field (overrides auto-detection).
148	
149	If detection fails or returns a non-semver value: note that version will default to `"1.0.0"` and the user can override in step 04.
150	
151	### 5. Report Analysis Summary
152	
153	Present the complete analysis:
154	
155	"**Analysis Complete**
156	
157	---
158	
159	**Target:** {repo URL or path}
160	**Language:** {detected language} ({confidence})
161	**Structure:** {file count} files across {directory count} directories
162	
163	**Key Modules ({count}):**
164	{bulleted list of modules}
165	
166	**Public Exports/Entry Points ({count}):**
167	{bulleted list of exports}
168	
169	**Notable Files:**
170	- README: {found/not found}
171	- Tests: {found/not found — location}
172	- Docs: {found/not found — location}
173	- Config: {list of config files found}
174	- Version: {detected version or "Not detected — defaulting to 1.0.0"}
175	
176	---
177	
178	{If language confidence is low:}
179	**Note:** Language detection confidence is low. You'll be able to override this in the next step.
180	
181	Moving to scope definition where you'll choose what to include and exclude."
182	
183	### 6. Auto-Proceed to Scope Definition
184	
185	Display: "**Proceeding to scope definition...**
186	
187	Review the analysis above. If anything looks wrong, let me know now — otherwise I'll proceed to scope definition."
188	
189	Pause briefly for user input. If the user provides corrections or asks questions, address them and re-present any updated analysis findings. Then proceed.
190	
191	#### Menu Handling Logic:
192	
193	- After analysis report is presented to user and any corrections addressed, load, read entire file, then execute {nextStepFile}
194	
195	#### EXECUTION RULES:
196	
197	- This is a soft auto-proceed step — present the pause prompt, wait briefly for user input
198	- If user provides corrections: address them, then proceed
199	- If no user input after a brief pause: proceed directly to step 03
200	
201	## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE
202	
203	ONLY WHEN the analysis is complete and the summary has been presented to the user will you load and read fully `./step-03-scope-definition.md` to begin scope definition.
204	
205

# Step 2: Analyze Target

## STEP GOAL:

To analyze the target repository by resolving its location, reading its structure, detecting the primary language, and listing top-level modules and exports — providing the user with a factual foundation for scoping decisions.

## Rules

- Focus only on analysis — do not define scope yet (Step 03)
- Do not make scoping decisions or recommendations
- Do not hallucinate or guess about repository contents

## MANDATORY SEQUENCE

**CRITICAL:** Follow this sequence exactly. Do not skip, reorder, or improvise unless user explicitly requests a change.

### 1. Resolve Target Location

**For GitHub URLs:**
- Use `gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}` to verify the repository exists
- Use `gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/git/trees/HEAD?recursive=1` to get the file tree

**Truncation detection:** After receiving the tree response, check the `truncated` field in the JSON output. If `truncated: true`:
- Display: "Note: GitHub API returned a truncated tree response ({count} items). Full analysis may require a local clone."
- Record in analysis summary: "Tree listing is partial — some files may not appear in the analysis."
- For very large repos (>1000 files in tree response): suggest local clone for complete analysis.

- If inaccessible: **HALT** — "**Error:** Cannot access repository at {url}. Please verify the URL is correct and you have access. If private, ensure `gh auth` is configured."

**For local paths:**
- Verify the directory exists
- List the directory tree
- If path doesn't exist: **HALT** — "**Error:** Directory not found at {path}. Please verify the path is correct."

Display: "**Resolving target...**"

### 2. Read Repository Structure

List the top-level directory structure:

"**Repository Structure:**
```
{repo-name}/
├── {top-level files}
├── {top-level directories}/
│   └── ...
└── ...
```
**Total:** {file count} files, {directory count} directories"

### 3. Detect Primary Language

Examine file extensions and configuration files to detect the primary language:

**Detection signals (check in order):**
1. `package.json` → JavaScript/TypeScript
2. `tsconfig.json` → TypeScript
3. `Cargo.toml` → Rust
4. `pyproject.toml` or `setup.py` or `setup.cfg` → Python
5. `go.mod` → Go
6. `pom.xml` or `build.gradle` → Java
7. `*.csproj` or `*.sln` → C#
8. `Gemfile` → Ruby
9. File extension frequency analysis as fallback

"**Detected language:** {language}
**Confidence:** {high/medium/low}
**Detection source:** {what config file or pattern confirmed it}"

If confidence is low or ambiguous: flag for user override in step 03.

### 4. List Top-Level Modules and Exports

Based on detected language, identify public API surface:

**For JavaScript/TypeScript:**
- Check `package.json` for `main`, `exports`, `module` fields
- Look for `index.ts`/`index.js` in `src/`
- List directories under `src/` as potential modules

**For Python:**
- Check `__init__.py` files for public exports
- List top-level packages under the source directory

**For Rust:**
- Check `lib.rs` for `pub mod` declarations
- List modules from `src/` directory

**For other languages:**
- List top-level source directories as potential modules
- Note any obvious entry points

"**Top-Level Modules/Directories:**
{numbered list of modules with brief description of each}

**Detected Exports/Entry Points:**
{numbered list of public-facing items found}"

**Semantic Signals (Forge+ and Deep with ccc only):**

**Remote source guard:** If the target source was resolved via GitHub API (remote URL, not a local file path), skip this CCC subsection — CCC requires a local source index and cannot operate on remote-only sources. Note: "CCC semantic discovery skipped — target is remote. CCC discovery will run automatically during create-skill after the source is cloned."

If `tools.ccc` is true in forge-tier.yaml, supplement the module listing with a semantic discovery pass:

**CCC Semantic Discovery:**
- **Claude Code:** Use `/ccc search "{repo_name} public API exports modules" {source_path}`
- **Cursor:** Use `ccc` MCP server `search` tool with query `"{repo_name} public API exports modules"` and path `{source_path}`
- **CLI fallback:** `ccc search "{repo_name} public API exports modules" --path {source_path} --limit 10`

See `knowledge/tool-resolution.md` for full bridge-to-tool mapping.

If results are returned, display:

"**Semantic Signals (ccc):**
{numbered list of file:snippet pairs from CCC results — top 5 most relevant}"

This supplements — never replaces — the explicit module list above. CCC may surface non-obvious entry points (dynamically constructed exports, re-export chains) that static directory analysis misses.

If CCC is unavailable or returns no results: skip this subsection silently.

### 4b. Detect Source Version

**If `target_version` was provided in step 01:**
- Display: "**Target version:** {target_version} (user-specified)"
- Still run auto-detection below for informational purposes.

Attempt to auto-detect the source version using the rules from the skill-brief-schema.md Version Detection section:

**For Python:** Check `pyproject.toml` `[project] version` (static) → if `dynamic = ["version"]`, check `__init__.py` for `__version__` → `_version.py` if exists → `setup.py` `version=` → `git describe --tags --abbrev=0`
**For JavaScript/TypeScript:** Check root `package.json` `"version"` field → if root has `"private": true` with a `"workspaces"` array or lacks a `"version"` field, fall back to a primary workspace package's `package.json` (e.g., `code/core/package.json`, or the first matching `packages/*/package.json`). For GitHub sources, prefer `gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/releases/latest` → `tag_name` when a non-pre-release tag exists, over a default-branch pre-release. Treat a version containing `-alpha`, `-beta`, `-rc`, `-next`, or `-canary` as a pre-release.
**For Rust:** Check `Cargo.toml` `[package] version` (static) → if `version = { workspace = true }`, resolve from workspace root `Cargo.toml` → `git describe --tags --abbrev=0`
**For Go:** Check `go.mod` or `git describe --tags --abbrev=0`

**For GitHub repos:** Use `gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/contents/{file}` to read version files (decode base64 content).
**For local repos:** Read the file directly.

Display: "**Detected version:** {version or 'Not detected — will default to 1.0.0'}"

{If target_version was provided AND auto-detected version differs:}
"**Note:** Detected version ({detected_version}) differs from your target version ({target_version}). Using target version."

{If target_version was provided:}
Store `target_version` as the brief's `version` field (overrides auto-detection).

If detection fails or returns a non-semver value: note that version will default to `"1.0.0"` and the user can override in step 04.

### 5. Report Analysis Summary

Present the complete analysis:

"**Analysis Complete**

---

**Target:** {repo URL or path}
**Language:** {detected language} ({confidence})
**Structure:** {file count} files across {directory count} directories

**Key Modules ({count}):**
{bulleted list of modules}

**Public Exports/Entry Points ({count}):**
{bulleted list of exports}

**Notable Files:**
- README: {found/not found}
- Tests: {found/not found — location}
- Docs: {found/not found — location}
- Config: {list of config files found}
- Version: {detected version or "Not detected — defaulting to 1.0.0"}

---

{If language confidence is low:}
**Note:** Language detection confidence is low. You'll be able to override this in the next step.

Moving to scope definition where you'll choose what to include and exclude."

### 6. Auto-Proceed to Scope Definition

Display: "**Proceeding to scope definition...**

Review the analysis above. If anything looks wrong, let me know now — otherwise I'll proceed to scope definition."

Pause briefly for user input. If the user provides corrections or asks questions, address them and re-present any updated analysis findings. Then proceed.

#### Menu Handling Logic:

- After analysis report is presented to user and any corrections addressed, load, read entire file, then execute {nextStepFile}

#### EXECUTION RULES:

- This is a soft auto-proceed step — present the pause prompt, wait briefly for user input
- If user provides corrections: address them, then proceed
- If no user input after a brief pause: proceed directly to step 03

## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE

ONLY WHEN the analysis is complete and the summary has been presented to the user will you load and read fully `./step-03-scope-definition.md` to begin scope definition.

# /// script
# requires-python = ">=3.11"
# dependencies = []
# ///
"""SKF Extract Public API — pure parser for manifest + entry-point content.

Takes a JSON payload on stdin describing one logical package (one
manifest, one or more entry-point files) and emits a structured
extraction inventory: package name, version, description, public
exports, declared dependencies, and (for Maven/Gradle) discovered
sub-modules. The helper does NO file I/O — the caller fetches files
(via gh api / web browsing / local filesystem / wherever) and pipes
their contents in. Multi-module monorepos are caller-orchestrated:
invoke once per module, aggregate the results.

Supported languages (--language values; first listed is preferred):

  js, ts, javascript, typescript    package.json     index.{js,ts}, src/index.{js,ts}
  python                            pyproject.toml   __init__.py / setup.py
  rust                              Cargo.toml       src/lib.rs
  go                                go.mod           top-level *.go
  java                              pom.xml          src/main/java/**/*.java
  kotlin                            build.gradle*    src/main/kotlin/**/*.kt

Mode flag (currently `quick` only; `full` reserved for skf-create-skill):

  --mode quick    Top-level exports only (one entry file per language).

Input JSON shape (stdin):

  {
    "language": "python",
    "manifest": {"path": "pyproject.toml", "content": "..."},
    "entries":  [{"path": "src/foo/__init__.py", "content": "..."}, ...],
    "mode":     "quick"
  }

Output JSON shape (stdout):

  {
    "language":    "python",
    "package_name": "foo",
    "version":      "1.2.3",
    "description":  "...",
    "exports":      [{"name": "Bar", "type": "class", "source_file": "..."}, ...],
    "dependencies": ["requests", "pydantic", ...],
    "modules":      ["server", "client"],         (Maven/Gradle only)
    "extra":        {"group_id": "com.example"}, (Maven only)
    "warnings":     ["..."]                        (parse failures, fallbacks)
  }

Exit codes:

  0    success
  1    payload-level error (unknown language, no manifest content)
  2    stdin / argparse / JSON-decode error
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import argparse
import json
import re
import sys
import tomllib
import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
from typing import Callable

# --------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Manifest parsers
# --------------------------------------------------------------------------


def parse_package_json(content: str) -> dict:
    try:
        data = json.loads(content)
    except json.JSONDecodeError as e:
        return {"_parse_error": f"package.json JSON parse error: {e}"}
    if not isinstance(data, dict):
        return {"_parse_error": "package.json root is not an object"}

39	
40	I'll help you define exactly what to skill and produce a `skill-brief.yaml` that drives the create-skill compilation workflow.
41	
42	We'll work through this together:
43	1. **Now:** Understand what you want to skill and why
44	2. **Next:** Analyze the target repo structure
45	3. **Then:** Define scope boundaries
46	4. **Finally:** Confirm and write the brief
47

50	{Else:}

52	
53	Let's get started."
54	
55	### 3. Gather Target Repository

57	"**What repository or documentation do you want to create a skill for?**
58	
59	Provide one of:
60	- A **GitHub URL** (e.g., `https://github.com/org/repo`)
61	- A **local path** (e.g., `/path/to/project`)
62	- **Documentation URLs** for a docs-only skill (e.g., `https://docs.stripe.com/api`) — use this when no source code is available (SaaS, closed-source)
63	
64	**Target:**"
65	
66	Wait for user response.
67	
68	**If user provides documentation URLs (not a repo):**
69	- Set `source_type: "docs-only"` in the brief data
70	- Collect one or more doc URLs with optional labels
71	- Note: `source_authority` will be forced to `community` (T3 external documentation)
72	- Note: `source_repo` becomes optional (can be set to the main doc site URL for reference)
73	
74	**If user provides a GitHub URL or local path:**

77	- If yes: collect doc URLs into `doc_urls`

81	- If maintainer: set `source_authority: "official"`
82	- If community user: set `source_authority: "community"` (default)
83	- If internal/proprietary: set `source_authority: "internal"`
84	
85	Default to `"community"` if user does not specify or skips. For `docs-only` skills, `source_authority` is always forced to `"community"`.
86	
87	Confirm the target.

89	### 3b. Gather Target Version

92	(Leave blank to auto-detect from source)"
93	
94	{If source_type is "docs-only":}

96	
97	Wait for user response.
98	
99	**If user provides a version:** Store as `target_version`. Set `version` to this value.
100	**If blank:** Proceed without `target_version` — version will be auto-detected in step 02.
101	
102	{If target_version was set AND doc_urls are being collected (either docs-only primary or supplemental):}
103	
104	"**You're targeting version {target_version}. Do these documentation URLs correspond to that version?** [Y/N]"
105	
106	- **If Y:** Proceed.
107	- **If N:** "Please provide the correct documentation URLs for version {target_version}." Re-collect doc_urls.
108	
109	### 4. Gather User Intent

112

115	- **Why** — what's the use case? How will an AI agent use this skill?

118	Take your time — the more context you share, the better the brief."
119	
120	Wait for user response. Ask follow-up questions if intent is unclear.
121

123	
124	If the user mentioned scope preferences in their intent response, acknowledge them:
125	
126	"**I noted these scope hints from your response:**
127	- {list any scope hints mentioned}
128	
129	We'll refine these after analyzing the repo structure in the next step."
130	
131	If no scope hints were mentioned, that's fine — skip this acknowledgment.
132	
133	### 6. Derive Skill Name
134	
135	Based on the target repo and intent, propose a skill name:
136	
137	"**Suggested skill name:** `{derived-name}` (kebab-case)
138	
139	This will be used for the output directory and file naming. Want to use this name or suggest something different?"

141	Wait for confirmation or alternative.

# Step 1: Gather Intent

## STEP GOAL:

To initialize the brief-skill workflow by discovering the forge tier configuration, then gathering the user's target repository, intent, and any upfront scope hints for skill creation.

## Rules

- Focus only on gathering intent — do not analyze the repo yet (Step 02)
- Do not examine source code or list exports in this step
- Open-ended discovery facilitation — collect target repo, user intent, scope hints, skill name

## MANDATORY SEQUENCE

**CRITICAL:** Follow this sequence exactly. Do not skip, reorder, or improvise unless user explicitly requests a change.

### 1. Discover Forge Tier

Attempt to load `{forgeTierFile}`:

**If found:**
- Read the tier level (quick, forge, forge+, or deep)
- Note available tools for scoping guidance later

**Apply tier override:** Read `{sidecar_path}/preferences.yaml`. If `tier_override` is set and is a valid tier value (Quick, Forge, Forge+, or Deep), use it instead of the detected tier.

**If not found:**
- "**Cannot proceed.** forge-tier.yaml not found at `{forgeTierFile}`. Please run the **setup** workflow first to configure your forge tier (Quick/Forge/Forge+/Deep)."
- HALT — do not proceed.

### 2. Welcome and Explain

"**Welcome to Brief Skill — the skill scoping workflow.**

I'll help you define exactly what to skill and produce a `skill-brief.yaml` that drives the create-skill compilation workflow.

We'll work through this together:
1. **Now:** Understand what you want to skill and why
2. **Next:** Analyze the target repo structure
3. **Then:** Define scope boundaries
4. **Finally:** Confirm and write the brief

{If tier override was applied:}
**Your forge tier:** {override tier} (overridden from {original tier}) — this determines what compilation capabilities are available.
{Else:}
**Your forge tier:** {detected tier} — this determines what compilation capabilities are available.

Let's get started."

### 3. Gather Target Repository

"**What repository or documentation do you want to create a skill for?**

Provide one of:
- A **GitHub URL** (e.g., `https://github.com/org/repo`)
- A **local path** (e.g., `/path/to/project`)
- **Documentation URLs** for a docs-only skill (e.g., `https://docs.stripe.com/api`) — use this when no source code is available (SaaS, closed-source)

**Target:**"

Wait for user response.

**If user provides documentation URLs (not a repo):**
- Set `source_type: "docs-only"` in the brief data
- Collect one or more doc URLs with optional labels
- Note: `source_authority` will be forced to `community` (T3 external documentation)
- Note: `source_repo` becomes optional (can be set to the main doc site URL for reference)

**If user provides a GitHub URL or local path:**
- Set `source_type: "source"` (default)
- Optionally ask: "Are there any documentation URLs you'd like to include for supplemental context? (These will be fetched as T3 external references.)"
- If yes: collect doc URLs into `doc_urls`

**Source authority (for all source-type skills):**
"**Are you the maintainer of this library, or creating a community skill?**"
- If maintainer: set `source_authority: "official"`
- If community user: set `source_authority: "community"` (default)
- If internal/proprietary: set `source_authority: "internal"`

Default to `"community"` if user does not specify or skips. For `docs-only` skills, `source_authority` is always forced to `"community"`.

Confirm the target.

### 3b. Gather Target Version

"**Are you targeting a specific version of this library?**
(Leave blank to auto-detect from source)"

{If source_type is "docs-only":}
"Since this is a docs-only skill with no source code, specifying the version is recommended — otherwise it defaults to 1.0.0."

Wait for user response.

**If user provides a version:** Store as `target_version`. Set `version` to this value.
**If blank:** Proceed without `target_version` — version will be auto-detected in step 02.

{If target_version was set AND doc_urls are being collected (either docs-only primary or supplemental):}

"**You're targeting version {target_version}. Do these documentation URLs correspond to that version?** [Y/N]"

- **If Y:** Proceed.
- **If N:** "Please provide the correct documentation URLs for version {target_version}." Re-collect doc_urls.

### 4. Gather User Intent

"**What's your intent for this skill?**

Help me understand:
- **What** specifically do you want to skill from this repo?
- **Why** — what's the use case? How will an AI agent use this skill?
- **Any initial thoughts** on scope? (Full library? Specific modules? Public API only?)

Take your time — the more context you share, the better the brief."

Wait for user response. Ask follow-up questions if intent is unclear.

### 5. Capture Scope Hints

If the user mentioned scope preferences in their intent response, acknowledge them:

"**I noted these scope hints from your response:**
- {list any scope hints mentioned}

We'll refine these after analyzing the repo structure in the next step."

If no scope hints were mentioned, that's fine — skip this acknowledgment.

### 6. Derive Skill Name

Based on the target repo and intent, propose a skill name:

"**Suggested skill name:** `{derived-name}` (kebab-case)

This will be used for the output directory and file naming. Want to use this name or suggest something different?"

Wait for confirmation or alternative.

### 7. Summarize Gathered Intent

"**Here's what I've captured:**

- **Target:** {repo URL or path}
- **Intent:** {user's intent summary}
- **Scope hints:** {any hints, or "None — we'll define scope after analysis"}
- **Skill name:** {confirmed name}
- **Source type:** {source or docs-only}
- **Source authority:** {official/community/internal}
{If target_version set:}
- **Target version:** {target_version} (user-specified)
{If doc_urls collected:}
- **Doc URLs:** {count} supplemental documentation URLs
- **Forge tier:** {tier}

Ready to analyze the target repository?"

### 8. Present MENU OPTIONS

Display: "**Select:** [C] Continue to Target Analysis"

#### Menu Handling Logic:

- IF C: Load, read entire file, then execute {nextStepFile}
- IF Any other: Help user, then [Redisplay Menu Options](#8-present-menu-options)

#### EXECUTION RULES:

- ALWAYS halt and wait for user input after presenting menu
- **GATE [default: use args]** — If `{headless_mode}`: consume pre-supplied arguments (target_repo, skill_name, scope_hint, language_hint) and auto-proceed. If required args missing, HALT: "headless mode requires target_repo and skill_name arguments."
- ONLY proceed to next step when user selects 'C'

## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE

ONLY WHEN C is selected and target repository is confirmed will you load and read fully `./step-02-analyze-target.md` to execute target analysis.

6	---
7	
8	# Step 3: Scope Definition
9	
10	## STEP GOAL:
11	
12	To collaboratively define the skill's inclusion and exclusion boundaries using the analysis findings from step 02, scope templates, and the user's intent from step 01.
13	
14	## Rules
15	
16	- Focus only on defining scope boundaries — do not write the brief yet (Step 05)
17	- Do not make scope decisions unilaterally — user drives all scope choices
18	- Produce: scope type, include patterns, exclude patterns
19	
20	## MANDATORY SEQUENCE
21	
22	**CRITICAL:** Follow this sequence exactly. Do not skip, reorder, or improvise unless user explicitly requests a change.
23	
24	### 1. Present Scope Context
25	
26	"**Let's define the scope for your skill.**
27	
28	Based on the analysis, here's what we're working with:
29	
30	- **Target:** {repo}
31	- **Language:** {detected language}
32	- **Modules found:** {count} — {list names}
33	- **Your intent:** {user intent from step 01}
34	{If scope hints from step 01:}
35	- **Your initial scope hints:** {hints}"
36	
37	### 2. Handle Docs-Only Mode (if applicable)
38	
39	**If `source_type: "docs-only"`:**
40	
41	"**Docs-only mode — scope is defined by documentation pages.**
42	
43	You've provided these documentation URLs:
44	{numbered list of doc_urls with labels}
45	
46	Which pages should be included in the skill? (Enter numbers, or 'all')
47	Any additional documentation URLs to add?"
48	
49	Wait for confirmation. Then skip to section 5 (Summarize Scope Decisions) with:
50	- `scope.type: "docs-only"`
51	- `scope.include`: confirmed doc URLs
52	- `scope.notes: "Generated from external documentation. All content is T3 confidence."`
53	
54	**If `source_type: "source"` (default):** Continue to scope templates below.
55	
56	### 2b. Confirm Supplemental Documentation (if doc_urls collected)
57	
58	**If `source_type: "source"` AND supplemental `doc_urls` were collected in step 01:**
59	
60	"**Supplemental documentation URLs:**
61	{numbered list of collected doc_urls with labels}
62	
63	These will be included as T3 external references in the skill brief.
64	Add, remove, or confirm these URLs."
65	
66	Wait for confirmation. Record any changes to `doc_urls`.
67	
68	**If no supplemental doc_urls were collected:** Skip this subsection.
69	
70	**Scope guidance for first-time users:** A well-scoped skill covers one cohesive capability with 3-8 primary functions. If the scope includes unrelated concerns (e.g., authentication AND data visualization), suggest splitting into separate briefs. If the scope is too narrow (single utility function), suggest expanding to the surrounding capability surface.
71	
72	### 2c. Offer Scope Templates
73	
74	Load `{scopeTemplatesFile}` for the scope type options ([F], [M], [P], [C], [R]) and their descriptions.
75	
76	Present: "**How broadly should this skill cover the library?**" followed by the scope type options from the loaded reference.
77	
78	Ask: "Which scope type fits your needs?"
79	
80	Wait for user selection.
81	
82	### 3. Define Boundaries Based on Selection
83	
84	Using the boundary definitions from `{scopeTemplatesFile}`, present the appropriate flow for the user's selected scope type ([F], [M], [P], [C], or [R]). Follow each type's prompts and wait for user input at each phase before proceeding.
85	
86	### 4. Handle Language Override
87	
88	{If language detection confidence was low from step 02:}
89	
90	"**Language confirmation needed.**
91	
92	The analysis detected **{language}** with low confidence. Is this correct, or should we set a different primary language?"
93	
94	Wait for confirmation or override.
95	
96	### 5. Summarize Scope Decisions
97	
98	"**Scope Summary:**
99	
100	**Type:** {Full Library / Specific Modules / Public API / Component Library / Reference App}
101	
102	**Include:**
103	{bulleted list of include patterns}
104	
105	**Exclude:**
106	{bulleted list of exclude patterns}
107	
108	**Language:** {confirmed language}
109	
110	{If any scope notes:}
111	**Notes:** {scope notes}
112	
113	Does this look right? You can adjust before we continue."
114	
115	Wait for confirmation. Make adjustments if requested.
116	
117	### 5b. Scripts & Assets Intent (Optional)
118	
119	**Only ask when `scope.type` is `full-library`, `specific-modules`, `component-library`, or `reference-app` (skip for `public-api` and `docs-only`). Reference apps routinely ship wiring scripts and build-config assets — prompt for them.**
120	
121	"Does this library include executable scripts (CLI tools, validation scripts, setup helpers) or static assets (config templates, JSON schemas, example configs) that should be packaged with the skill?"
122	
123	- **[D] Auto-detect** from source (default) — SKF will scan for `scripts/`, `bin/`, `assets/`, `templates/`, `schemas/` directories
124	- **[N] None expected** — skip script/asset detection
125	- Or describe what you expect (free text)
126	
127	Record the response as `scripts_intent` and `assets_intent` in the brief. Default to `detect` if user does not respond or skips.
128	
129	### 6. Present MENU OPTIONS
130	
131	Display: **Select an Option:** [A] Advanced Elicitation [P] Party Mode [C] Continue to Brief Confirmation
132	
133	#### Menu Handling Logic:
134	
135	- IF A: Invoke {advancedElicitationSkill}, and when finished redisplay the menu
136	- IF P: Invoke {partyModeSkill}, and when finished redisplay the menu
137	- IF C: Load, read entire file, then execute {nextStepFile}

file.content:
---
nextStepFile: './step-04-confirm-brief.md'
scopeTemplatesFile: 'assets/scope-templates.md'
advancedElicitationSkill: '/bmad-advanced-elicitation'
partyModeSkill: '/bmad-party-mode'
---

# Step 3: Scope Definition

## STEP GOAL:

To collaboratively define the skill's inclusion and exclusion boundaries using the analysis findings from step 02, scope templates, and the user's intent from step 01.

## Rules

- Focus only on defining scope boundaries — do not write the brief yet (Step 05)
- Do not make scope decisions unilaterally — user drives all scope choices
- Produce: scope type, include patterns, exclude patterns

## MANDATORY SEQUENCE

**CRITICAL:** Follow this sequence exactly. Do not skip, reorder, or improvise unless user explicitly requests a change.

### 1. Present Scope Context

"**Let's define the scope for your skill.**

Based on the analysis, here's what we're working with:

- **Target:** {repo}
- **Language:** {detected language}
- **Modules found:** {count} — {list names}
- **Your intent:** {user intent from step 01}
{If scope hints from step 01:}
- **Your initial scope hints:** {hints}"

### 2. Handle Docs-Only Mode (if applicable)

**If `source_type: "docs-only"`:**

"**Docs-only mode — scope is defined by documentation pages.**

You've provided these documentation URLs:
{numbered list of doc_urls with labels}

Which pages should be included in the skill? (Enter numbers, or 'all')
Any additional documentation URLs to add?"

Wait for confirmation. Then skip to section 5 (Summarize Scope Decisions) with:
- `scope.type: "docs-only"`
- `scope.include`: confirmed doc URLs
- `scope.notes: "Generated from external documentation. All content is T3 confidence."`

**If `source_type: "source"` (default):** Continue to scope templates below.

### 2b. Confirm Supplemental Documentation (if doc_urls collected)

**If `source_type: "source"` AND supplemental `doc_urls` were collected in step 01:**

"**Supplemental documentation URLs:**
{numbered list of collected doc_urls with labels}

These will be included as T3 external references in the skill brief.
Add, remove, or confirm these URLs."

Wait for confirmation. Record any changes to `doc_urls`.

**If no supplemental doc_urls were collected:** Skip this subsection.

**Scope guidance for first-time users:** A well-scoped skill covers one cohesive capability with 3-8 primary functions. If the scope includes unrelated concerns (e.g., authentication AND data visualization), suggest splitting into separate briefs. If the scope is too narrow (single utility function), suggest expanding to the surrounding capability surface.

### 2c. Offer Scope Templates

Load `{scopeTemplatesFile}` for the scope type options ([F], [M], [P], [C], [R]) and their descriptions.

Present: "**How broadly should this skill cover the library?**" followed by the scope type options from the loaded reference.

Ask: "Which scope type fits your needs?"

Wait for user selection.

### 3. Define Boundaries Based on Selection

Using the boundary definitions from `{scopeTemplatesFile}`, present the appropriate flow for the user's selected scope type ([F], [M], [P], [C], or [R]). Follow each type's prompts and wait for user input at each phase before proceeding.

### 4. Handle Language Override

{If language detection confidence was low from step 02:}

"**Language confirmation needed.**

The analysis detected **{language}** with low confidence. Is this correct, or should we set a different primary language?"

Wait for confirmation or override.

### 5. Summarize Scope Decisions

"**Scope Summary:**

**Type:** {Full Library / Specific Modules / Public API / Component Library / Reference App}

**Include:**
{bulleted list of include patterns}

**Exclude:**
{bulleted list of exclude patterns}

**Language:** {confirmed language}

{If any scope notes:}
**Notes:** {scope notes}

Does this look right? You can adjust before we continue."

Wait for confirmation. Make adjustments if requested.

### 5b. Scripts & Assets Intent (Optional)

**Only ask when `scope.type` is `full-library`, `specific-modules`, `component-library`, or `reference-app` (skip for `public-api` and `docs-only`). Reference apps routinely ship wiring scripts and build-config assets — prompt for them.**

"Does this library include executable scripts (CLI tools, validation scripts, setup helpers) or static assets (config templates, JSON schemas, example configs) that should be packaged with the skill?"

- **[D] Auto-detect** from source (default) — SKF will scan for `scripts/`, `bin/`, `assets/`, `templates/`, `schemas/` directories
- **[N] None expected** — skip script/asset detection
- Or describe what you expect (free text)

Record the response as `scripts_intent` and `assets_intent` in the brief. Default to `detect` if user does not respond or skips.

### 6. Present MENU OPTIONS

Display: **Select an Option:** [A] Advanced Elicitation [P] Party Mode [C] Continue to Brief Confirmation

#### Menu Handling Logic:

- IF A: Invoke {advancedElicitationSkill}, and when finished redisplay the menu
- IF P: Invoke {partyModeSkill}, and when finished redisplay the menu
- IF C: Load, read entire file, then execute {nextStepFile}
- IF Any other comments or queries: help user respond then [Redisplay Menu Options](#6-present-menu-options)

#### EXECUTION RULES:

- ALWAYS halt and wait for user input after presenting menu
- **GATE [default: C]** — If `{headless_mode}`: accept auto-detected scope (full-repo or manifest-based) and auto-proceed, log: "headless: using auto-detected scope"
- ONLY proceed to next step when user selects 'C'
- After other menu items execution, return to this menu
- User can chat or ask questions — always respond and then redisplay menu

## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE

ONLY WHEN C is selected and scope boundaries are confirmed will you load and read fully `./step-04-confirm-brief.md` to present the complete brief for confirmation.

I've verified the broken items and key findings against source — the report is accurate. Before executing, here's my proposed plan with commit ordering + risk notes. The work splits cleanly into 11 commits across 3 risk tiers.

## Plan: 11 commits

**Tier A — Low risk, prose-only** (8 commits, ~30 min)

| # | Scope | Files | Findings |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `fix(skf-brief-skill): prefix bare _bmad/ path with {project-root}` | step-06:3 | Broken #1 |
| 2 | `fix(skf-brief-skill): mark step-06 as terminal with completion sentinel` | step-06, SKILL.md Stages | Broken #2 |
| 3 | `feat(skf-brief-skill): pin language directive in every step's Rules block` | steps 01-06 | Theme 3 (7 obs) |
| 4 | `feat(skf-brief-skill): expand headless contract — args + JSON result + overwrite policy` | SKILL.md, step-01, step-03, step-05 | Theme 2 (4 obs) |
| 5 | `feat(skf-brief-skill): diagnostic-on-failure at HITL gates` | step-01, step-02, step-03 | Theme 4 (6 obs) |
| 6 | `refactor(skf-brief-skill): consolidate version resolution into references/version-resolution.md` | new ref, steps 01/02/05 | Theme 5 obs#2 |
| 7 | `feat(skf-brief-skill): add explicit description synthesis with confirmation` | step-01 §7, step-04 | Theme 5 obs#1 |
| 8 | `docs(skf-brief-skill): expand Overview with scoping theory + split rationale` | SKILL.md | R7 |

**Tier B — Medium risk, prose rewrite of step-02** (1 commit, 15 min)

| # | Scope | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| 9 | `refactor(skf-brief-skill): wire skf-extract-public-api.py into step-02` | Prose rewrite of step-02 §3/§4/§4b to delegate to existing script. Script already exists — verified at `src/shared/scripts/skf-extract-public-api.py`. Touches Theme 1 obs #1. |

**Tier C — Higher risk, new Python scripts with tests** (2 commits)

| # | Scope | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | `feat(shared): add render-skill-brief.py for deterministic YAML emission` | NEW script + PEP 723 + unit tests. Required by `scan-scripts.py`. Theme 1 obs #2,#4 |
| 11 | `feat(shared): add validate-skill-brief.py for post-write schema check` | NEW script + tests + wire into step-05. Theme 1 obs #3 |

## Decision points (need your call before I start)

1. **Commit 6 — preserved selections on step-04 [R] revise loop** (Theme 5 obs#3): I'll add a one-line note that prior selections are preserved; user re-confirms each at the existing menu. OK?

2. **Commit 9 — wiring strategy for `skf-extract-public-api.py`**: the script needs JSON-on-stdin (manifest content + entries content). The LLM must batch-read files first, then pipe. I'll keep step-02's narrative output, just delegate the parsing. OK?

3. **Tier C scope**: writing new scripts + unit tests is real engineering — could fail `npm run quality` in unexpected ways and inflate the PR. **Recommend:** ship Tier A+B as one PR (closes broken items + Themes 2/3/4/5 + the prose half of Theme 1), then split Tier C into a follow-up PR. The grade lifts to **Excellent** for everything except 3 of 4 Theme 1 observations, which are honestly script-design work that deserves its own review cycle.

   Or: I can do all 11 in one PR if you want. Which?

4. **Branch + PR**: per CONTRIBUTING.md, I'll branch `feat/skf-brief-skill-quality-fixes` (or `chore/skf-brief-skill-quality-fixes` — single conventional-commit isn't a clean fit). I'll run `npm run quality` after each commit and only push when fully green. OK?

5. **No party mode / advanced elicitation needed** — these are concrete, well-defined fixes with no decision-quality ambiguity.

Confirm: proceed with **Tier A+B (commits 1-9), defer Tier C to a follow-up**, and answer the 3 yes/no points above?

1	---
2	nextStepFile: './step-05-write-brief.md'
3	reviseStepFile: './step-03-scope-definition.md'
4	briefSchemaFile: 'assets/skill-brief-schema.md'
5	advancedElicitationSkill: '/bmad-advanced-elicitation'
6	partyModeSkill: '/bmad-party-mode'
7	---
8	
9	# Step 4: Confirm Brief
10	
11	## STEP GOAL:
12	
13	To present the complete skill brief in human-readable format, highlighting all fields that will be written to skill-brief.yaml, and obtain explicit user approval before writing.
14	
15	## Rules
16	
17	- Focus only on presenting and confirming — do not write files yet (Step 05)
18	- Do not proceed without explicit user approval (P2 confirmation gate)
19	
20	## MANDATORY SEQUENCE
21	
22	**CRITICAL:** Follow this sequence exactly. Do not skip, reorder, or improvise unless user explicitly requests a change.
23	
24	### 1. Load Schema
25	
26	Load `{briefSchemaFile}` to reference required fields and the human-readable presentation format.
27	
28	### 2. Assemble Complete Brief
29	
30	Compile all gathered data from steps 01-03 into the complete brief:
31	
32	- **name:** {skill name from step 01}
33	- **version:** {auto-detected from source, or "1.0.0" if not found — see schema for detection rules}
34	- **target_version:** {target_version from step 01, if set}
35	- **source_repo:** {target repo from step 01}
36	- **language:** {detected/confirmed language from steps 02-03}
37	- **description:** {derived from user intent in step 01}
38	- **forge_tier:** {tier from step 01}
39	- **created:** {current date}
40	- **created_by:** {user_name from config}
41	- **scope.type:** {scope type from step 03}
42	- **scope.include:** {include patterns from step 03}
43	- **scope.exclude:** {exclude patterns from step 03}
44	- **scope.notes:** {any scope notes from step 03}
45	- **source_type:** {source or docs-only, from step 01}
46	- **doc_urls:** {collected documentation URLs with labels, from steps 01/03 — include if source_type is "docs-only" or supplemental URLs were collected}
47	- **scripts_intent:** {detect/none/description from step 03, or "detect" if not explicitly set}
48	- **assets_intent:** {detect/none/description from step 03, or "detect" if not explicitly set}
49	- **source_authority:** {official/community/internal from step 01 — default "community"}
50	
51	### 3. Present Brief for Review
52	
53	Using the presentation format from the schema:
54	
55	"**Please review the complete skill brief before I write it.**
56	
57	---
58	
59	```
60	Skill Brief: {name}
61	====================
62	
63	Target:      {source_repo}
64	Language:    {language}
65	Forge Tier:  {forge_tier}
66	Description: {description}
67	
68	Scope: {scope.type}
69	  Include: {scope.include patterns, one per line}
70	  Exclude: {scope.exclude patterns, one per line}
71	  Notes:   {scope.notes}
72	
73	{If source_type is "docs-only":}
74	Source Type: docs-only
75	Doc URLs:
76	  {doc_urls, one per line with labels}
77	
78	{If source_type is "source" AND supplemental doc_urls collected:}
79	Supplemental Docs:
80	  {doc_urls, one per line with labels}
81	
82	{If scripts_intent or assets_intent was explicitly set (not default "detect"):}
83	Scripts:    {scripts_intent}
84	Assets:     {assets_intent}
85	
86	Source Authority: {source_authority}
87	
88	{If target_version is set:}
89	Target Version: {target_version} (user-specified)
90	Detected Version: {detected_version or "N/A"}
91	{Else:}
92	Version:    {version}
93	
94	Created:    {created}
95	Created by: {created_by}
96	```
97	
98	---"
99	
100	### 4. Highlight Items Needing Attention
101	
102	Flag any fields that may need review:
103	
104	{If language was overridden or low confidence:}
105	"**Note:** Language was {auto-detected / manually overridden}."
106	
107	{If description was derived (not stated by user):}
108	"**Note:** Description was derived from your stated intent. Adjust if needed."
109	
110	{If forge tier was defaulted:}
111	"**Note:** Forge tier defaulted to Quick (no forge-tier.yaml found)."
112	
113	{If any scope patterns seem broad or narrow:}
114	"**Note:** {specific observation about scope breadth}."
115	
116	{If target_version is set AND detected_version exists AND they differ:}
117	"**Note:** Target version ({target_version}) differs from detected source version ({detected_version}). The target version will be used for compilation."
118	
119	"**This is your last chance to make changes before writing the file.**
120	
121	You can:
122	- Adjust any field by telling me what to change
123	- Revise scope boundaries by selecting [R]
124	- Proceed to write by selecting [C]"
125	
126	### 5. Handle Inline Adjustments
127	
128	If the user requests changes to specific fields (name, description, version, etc.):
129	- Make the adjustment
130	- Re-present the updated brief
131	- Return to the menu
132	
133	### 6. Present MENU OPTIONS
134	
135	Display: **Select an Option:** [R] Revise Scope [A] Advanced Elicitation [P] Party Mode [C] Approve and Write
136	
137	#### Menu Handling Logic:
138	
139	- IF R: Load, read entire file, then execute {reviseStepFile} to re-enter scope definition
140	- IF A: Invoke {advancedElicitationSkill}, and when finished redisplay the menu
141	- IF P: Invoke {partyModeSkill}, and when finished redisplay the menu
142	- IF C: Load, read entire file, then execute {nextStepFile}
143	- IF Any other comments or queries: help user respond, apply any field adjustments, re-present brief if changed, then [Redisplay Menu Options](#6-present-menu-options)
144	
145	#### EXECUTION RULES:
146	
147	- ALWAYS halt and wait for user input after presenting menu
148	- **GATE [default: C]** — If `{headless_mode}`: auto-proceed with [C] Confirm, log: "headless: auto-confirm brief"
149	- ONLY proceed to write step when user selects 'C'
150	- After other menu items execution, return to this menu
151	- User can chat, request field changes, or ask questions — always respond and then redisplay menu
152	
153	## CRITICAL STEP COMPLETION NOTE
154	
155	ONLY WHEN C is selected and the user has explicitly approved the brief will you load and read fully `./step-05-write-brief.md` to write the skill-brief.yaml file.
